retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: "Cardiac enzymes" is a broad term encompassing several intracellular myocyte components that can be found in serum and measured under certain circumstances such as myocardial ischemia, trauma, myocarditis. In the proper clinical setting, elevation in the level of enzymes present in serum is key in the diagnosis of myocardial infarction. While troponin is the most commonly used cardiac enzyme for diagnosis of myocardial infarction, others exist and may be helpful in some situations.

retrieved_docs.title: Creatine Phosphokinase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated CK-MB levels have long indicated the diagnosis of AMI. Although many centers are utilizing troponin levels instead of CK-MB, a newer, more specific CK-MB analysis method is used. [43] The new testing method involves measuring the values of the CK-MB1 and CK-MB2 isoforms. In a normal patient, the ratio of CK-MB2 to CK-MB1 should be 1 to 1. In the case of AMI, the ratio peaks within 4 hours of the infarction. However, some evidence of AMI can be detected as early as 1 to 2 hours after the infarction. [44] To diagnose AMI, the ratio of CK-MB2 to CK-MB1 should be greater than 1.7 to 1. However, a ratio greater than or equal to 1.5 to 1 strongly indicates AMI. Additional cardiac conditions that increase serum CK and CK-MB in the serum are coronary artery bypass surgery, cardiac transplantation, myocarditis, and pulmonary embolism. [45] Serum CK activity is significantly elevated in all types of muscular dystrophy. [46] In Duchenne sex-linked muscular dystrophy, enzyme activity is highest in childhood and may be elevated long before clinical signs appear, especially during infancy (7 to 10 years of age).

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: The level of CK-MB, an isoenzyme of creatine kinase usually found in the myocardium, reaches its peak at 10 hours and normalizes within 2 to 3 days. CK-MB is also not clinically utilized due to its low specificity, rapid rise, and normalization. LDH levels reach their peak after 72 hours and return to normal within 10 to 14 days. In clinical practice, LDH is not used to diagnose acute MI.

retrieved_docs.title: Serum Myoglobin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Due to its relatively low molecular weight, myoglobin can leak from muscle tissue into the bloodstream following a skeletal or cardiac muscle injury, subsequently appearing in the urine. [8] Myoglobinemia and myoglobinuria have been used in the diagnosis of myopathies and cardiomyopathies. [28] A pronounced increase in serum myoglobin is an early quantitative indicator of acute myocardial infarction. [5] Myoglobinuria has been reported as a more sensitive clinical indicator of acute myocardial infarction compared to increased activities or concentrations of serum CK-MB isoenzyme, especially when blood samples are drawn in the early stage of the disease, such as within 3 hours after onset. [61] However, these assays are less specific because no assay can differentiate myocardial from skeletal muscle myoglobin. [62] Increased serum myoglobin concentrations return to normal sooner compared to total creatine kinase and the CK-MB isoenzyme in acute myocardial infarction. [63] The faster elimination of myoglobin from the blood may be advantageous in the assessment of reinfarctions occurring during the period of increased enzyme activities. [64] Recurrent increases in serum myoglobin are thus suggestive of an extension of the original infarction. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Creatine Kinase MB: Diagnostic Utility and Limitations -- Clinical Significance -- Non-Acute Myocardial Infarction Causes of CK-MB Elevation
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated CK-MB can result from skeletal muscle or myocardial cell death due to various causes. Other causes of CK-MB elevation in plasma include the following: False elevations: False elevations in CK-MB occur in the presence of atypical CK isoforms, macrokinases, and adenylate kinase; however, these false elevations can be eliminated by adding reagents to testing kits. [28] Cardiac etiology: Myocarditis and cardiac surgery can damage heart muscle, resulting in the elevation of CK-MB. Peripheral sources: Rhabdomyolysis, myositis, inflammatory myopathies, trauma, and medications, such as daptomycin, statins, and antiretrovirals, can also elevate CK-MB levels. To differentiate the elevation of CK-MB for cardiac etiology versus skeletal muscle source, the CK-MB relative index can be calculated using the formula: CK-MB relative index = CK-MB (ng/mL) × 100/CK (IU/L)

retrieved_docs.title: Creatine Phosphokinase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: CK activity is one of the markers of AMI. [40] The activity rises within 12 hours of AMI symptoms, peaks at 24 to 36 hours, and normalizes after 48 to 72 hours (see Image. Creatine Kinase Levels After a Myocardial Infarction). The issue with measuring CK activity for AMI is that it is non-specific to the heart. CK activity can increase in several conditions, such as rhabdomyolysis, chronic muscle diseases, burns, and strenuous exercise. [41] Although the CK-MB measurement is a diagnostic improvement over just CK, the enzyme can still increase in other conditions such as acute muscle injury, congestive cardiac failure, and arrhythmias. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Creatine Phosphokinase -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: In populations who frequently exercise, such as well-trained, long-distance runners, the CK-MB content of skeletal muscle increases, which may produce abnormal serum CK-MB concentrations to separate non-myocardial infarction from myocardial infarction patients; a “relative index” (RI) is necessary. [37] The RI relates CK-MB mass concentration in μg/L to measured total CK activity in U/L. [38] Results are expressed as a percentage: physiological: ≤3%, equivocal: 3% to 5%, consistent with myocardial necrosis: >5%. [39] Blood sampling between 8 and 36 hours from symptom onset is necessary to use the RI.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Ventricular Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac enzymes will be elevated in RVMI, similarly to LVMI scenarios. However, when differentiating between hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism (PE) and RVMI, the picture is not as clear, as both troponin-I and CK-MB levels may be elevated in a minority of cases of significant PE.

retrieved_docs.title: Creatine Kinase MB: Diagnostic Utility and Limitations -- Clinical Significance -- Diagnosis of Acute Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Before the advent of cardiac troponins, the CK-MB isoenzyme was the primary biochemical marker for diagnosing acute myocardial infarction. [27] CK-MB first appears 4 to 6 hours after the onset of symptoms, peaks at 24 hours, and returns to normal within 48 to 72 hours. Due to this short timeframe, its diagnostic value for acute myocardial infarction is limited both in the early stages (within the first few hours) and in the late stages (after 72 hours). However, its release kinetics can assist in diagnosing reinfarction if levels rise after initially declining following acute myocardial infarction. [9] The most common diagnostic criterion involved 2 serial elevations above the diagnostic cutoff level or a single result more than twice the upper limit of normal. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Biomarkers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac enzymes have been in use since the mid-20th century in evaluating patients with suspected acute myocardial infarction (MI). The biomarkers used back then are not clinically relevant today as more sensitive and specific biomarkers have replaced them. [3] Troponins are the key cardiac biomarkers in modern medicine for diagnosing acute myocardial ischemia. [4] In contrast to creatine kinase (CK), which usually elevates 6 to 12 hours after arriving at the emergency department, troponins show elevation in most AMI cases within 2 to 3 hours of arrival. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Diagnostic Tests -- Creatine Kinase/CK-MB
retrieved_docs.content: Creatine kinase is found in all muscle tissues and is nonspecific for myocyte injury; however, CK-MB is relatively specific for myocardial tissue. CK-MB can be found in serum within 4 to 6 hours of the onset of myocardial ischemia; however, it can take up to 12 hours in some patients. CK-MB levels return to baseline within 36 to 48 hours and, therefore, are sometimes still used to assess for reinfarction after the intervention. Elevations in CK-MB must be interpreted with caution in situations where skeletal muscle injury or disease is also suspected, as CK-MB is released from damaged skeletal muscle. Some institutions will report a ratio of CK/MB to CK to ascertain whether elevations in CK-MB are increased to an extent greater than what would be expected with skeletal muscle injury alone; however, these ratios or indexes have not been demonstrated to improve sensitivity or specificity regarding the diagnosis of myocardial ischemia. CK-MB levels alone are most helpful in situations where myocardial ischemia is suspected and skeletal muscle injury or disease is not suspected. As discussed below, however, troponin is preferred in almost all situations where it is available for use.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Diagnostic Tests -- Troponin
retrieved_docs.content: In most clinical settings, cardiac troponin is the cardiac enzyme of choice, and other enzymes should not be routinely used. There are many reasons for this, but ultimately, troponin has been shown to be more specific and more sensitive to cardiac injury. Nearly all false-positive troponins are limited to situations where there is antibody cross-reactivity within the testing assay, as troponin is not released from damaged skeletal muscle. CK-MB is released from skeletal muscle, and this can lead to falsely positive elevation. Per gram of myocardial tissue, more troponin is present than CK-MB.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation -- Biomarker Detection of MI
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac troponins (I and T) are components of the contractile apparatus of myocardial cells and expressed almost exclusively in the heart. Elevated serum levels of cardiac troponin are not specific to the underlying mode of injury (ischemic vs. tension) [14] [20] . The rising and/or falling pattern of cardiac troponins (cTn) values with at least one value above the 99 percentile of upper reference limit (URL) associated with symptoms of myocardial ischemia would indicate an acute MI. Serial testing of cTn values at 0 hours, 3 hours, and 6 hours would give a better perspective on the severity and time course of the myocardial injury. Depending on the baseline cTn value, the rising/falling pattern is interpreted. If the cTn baseline value is markedly elevated, a minimum change of greater than 20% in follow up testing is significant for myocardial ischemia. Creatine kinase MB isoform can also be used in the diagnosis of MI, but it is less sensitive and specific than cTn level. [4] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Diagnostic Tests -- Creatine Kinase/CK-MB
retrieved_docs.content: Creatine kinase is a cytosolic protein involved with mitochondrial phosphate transport.   CK exists in three different dimer configurations (MM, MB, BB) of two CK isoenzymes, M and B. Prior to the ubiquitous use of troponin, CK-MB was the mainstay cardiac enzyme for the diagnosis of myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The morbidity and mortality associated with acute myocardial infarction are well understood and discussed elsewhere. Given the known morbidity and mortality associated with acute myocardial infarction and the importance of early diagnosis and management, the above definition places a heavy burden on cardiac enzymes as their elevation alone, along with symptoms of ischemia, is enough to make the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Biomarkers -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: CK-MB still holds some diagnostic value in cardiac and noncardiac conditions. CK-MB is detected in the serum 4 hours after myocardial injury, peaks by 24 hours, and normalizes within 48 to 72 hours. [28] CK-MB is a useful biomarker for detecting AMI as it has a relative specificity for cardiac tissue but can still become elevated in noncardiac conditions, such as skeletal muscle injury, hypothyroidism, chronic renal failure, and severe exercise. [29] The ratio of CK-MB2 to CK-MB1 ≥ 1.5 and a CK-MB relative index (CK-MB/total CK x 100) ≥ 2.5 improve cardiac tissue specificity and indicate acute MI. [30] As CK-MB levels typically normalize within 48 to 72 hours after myocardial ischemia (in contrast to troponins, which can persist for days), monitoring CK-MB can be beneficial in identifying if levels rise again after initially declining. [28]

retrieved_docs.content: artery, intermediate artery). Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

retrieved_docs.content: further emphasizes the importance of recognizing valvular pathology when evaluating and managing cardiac patients. Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2247
retrieved_docs.content: Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of anterior descending branch of left coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distal portion of right coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of mid portion of right coronary artery, Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of posterior descending branch of right coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, Acute non‑Q wave infarction, Acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Acute widespread myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction all share the same anatomic finding site: the myocardium (myokard). This common localization indicates that, irrespective of the specific coronary artery involved, the presence or absence of ST‑segment elevation, Q‑wave formation, or the extent of the infarction, the primary tissue affected is the myocardial muscle. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities all reflect myocardial injury guides clinicians to focus diagnostic imaging, biomarker assessment, and therapeutic strategies on preserving myocardial function and limiting infarct size.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of distal portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of mid portion of right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving left anterior descending coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery, acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, and acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder) all share the myocardium as their primary site of pathology. Regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern (Q wave, ST‑segment elevation, or non‑ST‑segment elevation) or the particular coronary artery segment that is occluded (proximal, mid, distal, left anterior descending, circumflex, or right coronary artery), the underlying tissue injury occurs within the myocardial muscle. This common anatomic focus underscores that, in clinical assessment and therapeutic planning, the detection of myocardial injury—whether by ECG changes, cardiac biomarkers, or imaging—remains central to the diagnosis and management of all forms of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2257
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is a cardiac event in which the myocardium (myokard) is the primary site of injury, and it can be classified by electrocardiographic patterns, anatomic distribution, and precipitating circumstances. Q‑wave infarctions represent a form of acute MI characterized by pathologic Q waves on the ECG, while ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) are identified by elevated ST segments and are further subdivided according to the coronary artery segment that is occluded. Occlusion of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, including its proximal, mid, distal, septal, and marginal branches, produces anterior‑wall infarctions; blockage of the right coronary artery (RCA) and its branches—such as the posterior descending, marginal, intermediate, and distal portions—results in inferior or right‑sided infarctions; and interruption of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery leads to lateral‑wall involvement. Non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (NSTEMI) denote a subtype in which ST elevation is absent but myocardial necrosis is present. Widespread or extensive myocardial infarctions describe cases where a large territory of the myocardium is affected, often reflecting multiple or proximal arterial occlusions. Additionally, acute myocardial infarction can occur iatrogenically during invasive cardiac procedures, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring and rapid intervention. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved and the ECG pattern guides diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decisions, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal finding site across these varied presentations aids clinicians in correlating ECG findings, angiographic territories, and clinical contexts to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: tissue in the pathophysiology and evaluation of AMI. Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

This consistent anatomic involvement underscores the central role of the myokard in the clinical presentation, diagnostic evaluation, and therapeutic management of all forms of acute myocardial infarction.

In addition, acute infarct morphology is also associated with Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate that acute infarct serves as the common pathological substrate linking diverse coronary occlusion sites and both ST‑segment elevation and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction presentations, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is classified as encompassing the subgroup of thrombotic and arterial embolism cases (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية, SMQ), indicating that these thrombotic and embolic arterial events are considered members of the acute myocardial infarction category. This classification aids in the systematic organization of cardiovascular diagnoses and informs clinical assessment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2405
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the specific forms caused by occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and involvement of the left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are all distinct clinical entities that share a common anatomic focus: the myocardium. Each of these conditions represents an acute loss of blood supply to myocardial tissue, whether the injury is precipitated by specific coronary artery branch occlusions, surgical or procedural contexts, or broader patterns of infarction. Recognizing that all of these varied presentations localize to the myocardium underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the numerous subtypes caused by occlusion of the left coronary artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑Q wave infarction, and postoperative acute myocardial infarction all share the same anatomic finding site: the myocardium (myokard). This indicates that regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern, coronary artery involved, or clinical context, the primary tissue affected in these acute coronary syndromes is the myocardial muscle. Recognizing the myocardium as the common locus of injury is essential for diagnosis, imaging, and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving cardiac function across the diverse presentations of acute myocardial infarction.
